questions.prompt: Your support chatbot answers routine questions in 2 seconds. A teammate adds a planning step 'to make it smarter' and latency jumps to 6 seconds with no measurable quality gain. Right move?

choices.text: Drop planning on routine questions. Reserve plan-then-act for genuinely complex queries (5+ tool calls, deep dependencies, costly wrong sequences). Detect complexity with a small classifier or simple heuristics; route easy ones to plain react. Best of both worlds.

questions.explanation: Plan-then-act earns its cost on complex multi-step problems where wrong sequences are expensive. On routine queries it's pure latency overhead. Use intent-based routing: easy → react, complex → plan-then-act. Same agent, different loops based on task shape.

questions.prompt: What's the production sweet spot for agent loop architecture?

choices.text: Plan the outline, react within each step. Macro structure from planning; micro flexibility from react. Surface the plan to users so they can edit/abort. Re-plan only when observations contradict the plan — not on every step.

questions.explanation: Plan-then-react is the canonical production pattern for non-trivial agents. The plan gives the user a preview and the agent a structure; react inside each step handles the details. Re-planning is reserved for the cases where the world contradicts the plan — not as a constant overhead.
